claims.claim_text: A September 2023 EKS incident at a major video streaming company scaled from 200 pods to 1,800 pods in 20 minutes.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening sentence
claims.anchor_text: In September 2023, a major video streaming company running on EKS launched a highly anticipated new series that rapidly went viral. Within twenty minutes, their backend encoding and delivery services scaled from 200 pods to 1,800 pods.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large EKS-backed media launches can trigger sudden pod-count spikes that stress node provisioning if cluster capacity does not scale quickly enough.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with named timing and scale details that need a real source or removal.

claims.claim_text: The autoscaling path took 8 minutes to process pending pods, 3 minutes to scale ASGs, and 2 minutes for EC2 bootstrap.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, second paragraph
claims.anchor_text: It took 8 minutes to iterate through pending pods, 3 minutes to evaluate and scale the underlying Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs), and another 2 minutes for the EC2 instances to bootstrap and join the cluster.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The node-scaling path introduced several sequential delays across scheduling, capacity selection, and instance startup.
claims.rationale: These are precise incident timings with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Users experienced 13 continuous minutes of degraded service.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, third paragraph
claims.anchor_text: By the time infrastructure capacity caught up to application demand, users had experienced 13 continuous minutes of degraded service.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By the time infrastructure capacity caught up to application demand, users had already experienced a sustained period of degraded service.
claims.rationale: The exact duration is part of the same unsourced outage narrative.

claims.claim_text: The spike cost about $12,000 in one day because it ran entirely on On-Demand instances.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, third paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Because the scaling occurred entirely on On-Demand instances, the 1,800-pod spike incurred approximately $12,000 in compute costs for a single day.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Because the spike ran on On-Demand capacity, it also drove a substantial short-term compute bill.
claims.rationale: This is false precision for a historical price outcome without a verifiable billing source.

claims.claim_text: After the incident, the team replaced Cluster Autoscaler with Karpenter and cut capacity availability to under 90 seconds with no user-facing degradation.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, closing paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Following the incident, the engineering team modernized their cluster architecture by replacing Cluster Autoscaler with Karpenter, implementing native Spot instance orchestration, and deploying OpenCost for granular namespace attribution. During subsequent traffic spikes, Karpenter detected unschedulable pods, formulated an optimized instance mix, and invoked the EC2 Fleet API directly. Compute capacity became available in under 90 seconds, completely eliminating user-facing degradation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams that modernize EKS node provisioning with Karpenter, broader capacity choices, and better cost visibility can reduce scaling delays and improve resilience during later traffic spikes.
claims.rationale: This is the payoff half of the same unattributed incident story and keeps uncited exact outcomes.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter is an open-source, high-performance Kubernetes autoscaling project built by AWS.
claims.span_hint: section: Karpenter: Next-Generation Node Provisioning, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS’s launch post directly describes Karpenter as an open-source, high-performance cluster autoscaler built with AWS.

claims.claim_text: Cluster Autoscaler takes 3-10 minutes while Karpenter takes 30-90 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Karpenter vs Cluster Autoscaler table, Provisioning speed row
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Provisioning speed** | Typically slower because it scales node groups | Typically faster because it provisions capacity directly |
claims.rationale: The exact timing ranges are not backed by an authoritative primary source in the cited docs.

claims.claim_text: NodePools define constraints and preferences for Karpenter-managed nodes, and higher weight means higher priority when multiple NodePools match.
claims.span_hint: section: NodePool: Defining Constraints; On-Demand Fallback Pattern
claims.rationale: The primary Karpenter NodePools documentation covers constraints and weight behavior, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter falls through to the On-Demand NodePool only on an InsufficientInstanceCapacity error after strictly evaluating weight.
claims.span_hint: section: On-Demand Fallback Pattern, paragraph after NodePool examples
claims.anchor_text: Karpenter evaluates `weight` strictly. The primary `compute-spot` NodePool forces an attempt on the Spot market. Only upon an `InsufficientInstanceCapacity` error from the AWS API will Karpenter fall through to the On-Demand NodePool.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Karpenter uses NodePool weight as a preference signal. If compatible Spot offerings are unavailable, it can move on to an On-Demand NodePool.
claims.rationale: The exact fallback trigger and error wording are more specific than the source material directly supports.

claims.claim_text: EKS can export five control plane log types to CloudWatch Logs, and they are disabled by default.
claims.span_hint: section: Control Plane Logging; log type table
claims.rationale: The EKS user guide lists the five log types and states that control plane logs are off by default.

claims.claim_text: A cluster generating 50 GB/day of audit logs costs about $25/day in CloudWatch ingestion.
claims.span_hint: section: Control Plane Logging, stop-and-think note
claims.anchor_text: An active cluster generating 50 GB/day of audit logs costs ~$25/day in CloudWatch ingestion alone.
claims.suggested_rewrite: An active cluster with heavy audit logging can generate noticeable CloudWatch ingestion costs, so log selection should be deliberate.
claims.rationale: The exact dollar figure is derived from current pricing and a hypothetical workload, so it should be framed as an illustration rather than a fixed fact.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch Container Insights collects EKS cluster, node, pod, and related metrics in the ContainerInsights namespace.
claims.span_hint: section: Container Insights: Node and Pod Metrics; metrics table
claims.rationale: AWS documents the ContainerInsights namespace and the EKS metrics available there.

claims.claim_text: High-cardinality clusters inevitably make Container Insights prohibitively expensive and the industry standard mandates moving to Prometheus.
claims.span_hint: section: Prometheus Integration, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: While Container Insights offers a seamless zero-ops experience, high-cardinality clusters will inevitably encounter prohibitive cost scaling. At scale, the industry standard mandates transitioning to a Prometheus-backed observability pipeline.
claims.suggested_rewrite: While Container Insights is convenient, some teams choose Prometheus-based pipelines as clusters and observability requirements grow.
claims.rationale: The original wording makes a strong universal cost and industry-standards claim that is not directly supported by a primary source.

claims.claim_text: Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus provides managed workspaces with remote-write and query endpoints for Prometheus metrics.
claims.span_hint: section: Amazon Managed Prometheus (AMP)
claims.rationale: AWS’s AMP documentation describes workspace creation and remote-write/query endpoints.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost allocates Kubernetes infrastructure costs across dimensions such as labels, namespaces, pods, and clusters.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Allocation with Kubecost and OpenCost; OpenCost (Open Source)
claims.rationale: The OpenCost specification is the right primary source, but its host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Kyverno can enforce required labels at admission time using validate rules and enforce/audit actions.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Allocation Strategies, paragraph after labeling example
claims.rationale: Kyverno’s validate-rule docs directly support the admission-enforcement behavior, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The example cost report’s $4,100 idle or unallocated spend highlights a major savings opportunity.
claims.span_hint: section: Reading a Cost Report, sentence after table
claims.anchor_text: In this scenario, the idle/unallocated sum ($4,100) highlights a massive surface area for savings through tighter consolidation policies or more aggressive resource right-sizing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In this scenario, a large idle or unallocated share would highlight a clear opportunity for savings through tighter consolidation policies or more aggressive resource right-sizing.
claims.rationale: The precise dollar figure is illustrative example math, not a source-backed external fact.

claims.claim_text: The control plane should be upgraded before worker nodes because kubelet must not be newer than kube-apiserver under Kubernetes version-skew rules.
claims.span_hint: section: EKS Upgrade Runbooks, stop-and-think note
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes version skew policy explicitly defines the control-plane-before-kubelet compatibility rule.

claims.claim_text: VPC CNI, CoreDNS, and kube-proxy should be moved to the exact builds verified against v1.35.
claims.span_hint: section: EKS Upgrade Runbooks, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Core Add-on Upgrades**: Transition VPC CNI, CoreDNS, and kube-proxy components to the exact builds verified against v1.35.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Core Add-on Upgrades**: Update VPC CNI, CoreDNS, and kube-proxy to the Amazon EKS-recommended versions for your cluster version.
claims.rationale: The exact-build wording is stronger than the cited EKS upgrade guidance, which speaks in terms of recommended add-on versions.

claims.claim_text: Using amiSelectorTerms with the latest alias automatically triggers seamless rolling node replacement through drift mechanics.
claims.span_hint: section: EKS Upgrade Runbooks, item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Data Plane (Node) Rotation**: Karpenter streamlines this exponentially. By mapping the `amiSelectorTerms` to the latest alias, node drift mechanics automatically initiate a rolling replacement across the fleet seamlessly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Data Plane (Node) Rotation**: Karpenter can simplify node rotation when your AMI selection and disruption settings are configured to replace drifted nodes on newer images.
claims.rationale: The source material supports dynamic AMI selection but not the stronger claim of seamless automatic fleet-wide rolling replacement as written.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter evaluates over 600 EC2 instance types and refreshes pricing from the EC2 pricing API every 6 hours.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. Karpenter evaluates over 600 EC2 instance types when deciding what to launch. For each set of pending pods, it simulates packing them onto every compatible instance type, calculates the cost, and selects the cheapest option. This evaluation happens in milliseconds thanks to an in-memory instance type database that Karpenter refreshes from the EC2 pricing API every 6 hours.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. Karpenter considers a broad set of compatible instance types for a pending pod batch and passes multiple viable options to EC2 Fleet instead of relying on a single fixed node group.
claims.rationale: Current Karpenter documentation supports a broader principle here but not these exact numbers and refresh cadence.

claims.claim_text: Audit logs for a 500-pod cluster can reach 10-15 GB/day, cost $5-7.50/day at $0.50/GB, and filtering cuts volume by 80%.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 2
claims.anchor_text: 2. EKS control plane audit logs record every single API request, including the request body and response. For a cluster with 500 pods and active HPA/VPA controllers, the audit log volume can reach 10-15 GB per day. At CloudWatch's $0.50/GB ingestion rate, that is $5-7.50/day or $150-225/month just for audit log storage. Many teams filter audit logs to only capture write operations and authentication events, reducing volume by 80%.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. EKS audit logs can become expensive in busy clusters because they record detailed API activity, so teams often scope log collection to the signals they actually need for security and debugging.
claims.rationale: The exact workload, volume, savings, and cost numbers are not backed by a directly matching primary source.

claims.claim_text: Specific Spot interruption rates for m5.xlarge and m6i.xlarge in us-east-1, plus diversification lowering effective interruption below 2%, are given as 2024 facts.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. Spot instance interruption rates vary dramatically by instance type and region. In 2024 data, the `m5.xlarge` in us-east-1 had a roughly 5% monthly interruption rate, while the `m6i.xlarge` in the same region was under 3%. By diversifying across 15-20 instance types and 3 AZs, you can achieve an effective interruption rate below 2% per node per month, making Spot viable even for user-facing services behind proper redundancy.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. Spot interruption behavior varies by instance pool and region, so teams reduce risk by diversifying instance types and Availability Zones and by building applications to tolerate interruptions.
claims.rationale: AWS provides broader Spot interruption guidance, but not these exact per-instance and effective-rate numbers.

claims.claim_text: Kubecost found average cluster utilization of only 20-35% across thousands of clusters, implying 65-80% of spend is wasted.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. Kubecost analysis across thousands of Kubernetes clusters found that the average cluster utilization (actual CPU/memory usage vs provisioned) is only 20-35%. This means 65-80% of infrastructure spend is wasted on idle resources. The three highest-impact optimizations are: (1) right-sizing pod resource requests using VPA recommendations, (2) enabling Karpenter consolidation to remove underutilized nodes, and (3) using Spot instances for stateless and fault-tolerant workloads.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. Many Kubernetes clusters have substantial room to improve utilization, so right-sizing requests, consolidating underused nodes, and moving suitable workloads to Spot can materially reduce spend.
claims.rationale: These percentages are precise vendor study numbers without a directly attributable primary source in the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Running Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter simultaneously causes them to race over node lifecycle and is not recommended.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, first row
claims.rationale: AWS’s Karpenter launch post explicitly warns against running Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter together because they can race on unschedulable pods.

further_reading.why_relevant: This is the current Amazon EKS entry point for supported autoscaling options and Karpenter context.

further_reading.title: Send control plane logs to CloudWatch Logs
further_reading.why_relevant: It documents the five EKS control-plane log types, defaults, and operational caveats.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Version Skew Policy
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the canonical reference for control-plane and node upgrade order compatibility.

notes: Quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, and code blocks were excluded per the task rules; table and prose claims were only captured where they carried source-sensitive factual assertions.
